paper_title: Comparing methods for controlled capture and quantification of pollen in Cannabis sativa

methods.description: This method describes the experimental setup and protocols used to compare the yield and efficiency of different pollen collection methods (Hand, Bag, Water, Vacuum) from Cannabis sativa plants over multiple collection events.

steps.description: In the first trial, we used three pollen collection methods (also referred to as collection treatments): hand collection (control), bag collection, and water collection ( Fig. 1). In the second trial, we maintained hand collection as a control and tested vacuum collection.
steps.searchable_summary: Design and set up experimental trials comparing different pollen collection methods
inputs: List of collection methods (Hand, Bag, Water, Vacuum)

steps.description: Cannabis anthers dehisce non-concurrently, and as such we initiated pollen collection when at least 33% of visible male flowers were releasing pollen (this date was termed day zero) to ensure there was enough pollen to collect in the context of a breeding program.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and mark the start date (day zero) for pollen collection based on anther dehiscence percentage
outputs: List of plants ready for collection

steps.description: From each plant, we performed three collections over a seven-day period, where initial sufficient anther dehiscence occurred on day zero, the first collection occurred on day 1, and subsequent collections on days 4 and 7.
steps.searchable_summary: Schedule and perform repeated pollen collections on days 1, 4, and 7 relative to day zero
outputs: Completed collection events for each plant

steps.description: during hand collection, we placed a 50-mL centrifuge vial (Falcon 50-mL centrifuge tubes; Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) at the base of inflorescences with dehiscent anthers and used tweezers to tap or brush the dehiscent flowers, allowing pollen to fall into the collection vial.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect pollen using the hand tapping/brushing method into a centrifuge vial

steps.description: During the water collection method, we flipped plants upside down and dipped them into a 100-mL graduated cylinder filled with 50 mL of distilled water to wash all the pollen off the plant. The water sample containing pollen was then transferred into a 50-mL centrifuge vial for storage.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect pollen using the water washing method by dipping the plant and transferring the water to a vial

steps.description: During bag collection, we placed brown paper bags (41 cm × 11 cm × 5 cm) on the plants and loosely tied the base of the bag using twine. We then flipped the plants upside down and lightly shook them for 10 s to encourage dehiscence of pollen, after which we untied the twine from the base of the bag to remove the plant, and quickly sealed the bag to prevent pollen loss.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect pollen using the bag shaking method by bagging the plant, shaking, and sealing the bag
inputs: Brown paper bag (41 cm × 11 cm × 5 cm)
outputs: Pollen sample in paper bag (Bag method)

steps.description: We retrofitted small paper cups (9 oz, DR-1000765, Dixie; Koch Industries, Wichita, Kansas, USA) to act as filters inside a hand-held vacuum (4-W mini portable vacuum; Honk Electronic Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, China) by reducing the height of the cup to 2 cm and poking four holes (using a hole punch) along the cup's sides (0.63-cm diameter) to allow a small amount of airflow through the filter. Retrofitted cup filters were then placed inside the body of the vacuum, between the nozzle and the motor, intercepting and storing all of the vacuumed particles.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare filter cups for vacuum pollen collection by modifying paper cups
outputs: Retrofitted paper cup filters for vacuum

steps.description: Pollen was then collected by vacuuming the leaves and inflorescences of male plants, after which the filter was carefully removed and transferred to plastic containers for storage.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect pollen using the vacuum method with a filter cup and transfer the filter to a container
inputs: Hand-held vacuum with retrofitted filter cup
outputs: Pollen sample transferred to plastic container (Vacuum method)

steps.description: All pollen samples were stored at −20°C after collection.

methods.description: This method describes the statistical procedures used to analyze the collected pollen data, including correlating spectroscopy readings with pollen counts and comparing the effectiveness of different collection methods using ANOVA and MANOVA.

steps.description: All analyses were run in R version 3.6.0 (using the stats package 2019-04-06; R Core Team, 2019).
steps.searchable_summary: Initialize R environment for data analysis using specified version and package

steps.description: To test if spectroscopy readings were correlated with microscopy-derived pollen count data, we used a linear regression model. We used the adjusted R 2 value and correlation of the linear model to evaluate how well reflectance predicts pollen counts.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform linear regression to correlate spectroscopy readings with microscopy pollen counts
outputs: Linear regression model results (adjusted R^2, correlation coefficient)

steps.description: We log-transformed the response variables, i.e., the spectroscopy reading (yield) and efficiency (yield divided by time spent collecting), to satisfy the assumption of normally distributed residuals.
steps.searchable_summary: Log-transform pollen yield (spectroscopy reading) and collection efficiency data

steps.description: We then compared transformed estimates of pollen yield and collection efficiency with a repeated-measures multivariate ANOVA (MANOVA) (analyzing both yield and efficiency as the response variables) followed by individual repeated-measures ANOVAs (analyzing yield and efficiency as response variables independently) when factors were found to be statistically significant, using the manova() and aov() functions.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform repeated-measures MANOVA on log-transformed yield and efficiency data

steps.description: followed by individual repeated-measures ANOVAs (analyzing yield and efficiency as response variables independently) when factors were found to be statistically significant, using the manova() and aov() functions.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform individual repeated-measures ANOVAs for yield and efficiency if MANOVA factors are significant
outputs: ANOVA results for efficiency (statistical significance of factors)

steps.description: Any experimental factors that were determined to be statistically significant underwent subsequent post-hoc analysis using Tukey's honest significant difference test (using the TukeyHSD() function in the R stats package, version 3.6.0).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform post-hoc Tukey's HSD test for statistically significant factors from ANOVAs
